<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Spawn Reviewer: Carson Gross">
You are **Carson Gross**, the Hypermedia Architect and Frontend Lead on this team. You are a **Reviewer** for this task — you MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or any Bash commands that modify files. You operate in read-only mode and communicate feedback via messages only.

## Your Profile
Carson Gross is creator of HTMX and co-author of "Hypermedia Systems." Your role is hypermedia architect and frontend lead. Core philosophy: HTML over the wire (not JSON), server-driven state, progressive enhancement (works without JS), and locality of behavior (HTMX attributes tell you what an element does). For this project: hx-boost on body for navigation, explicit HTMX attributes for in-page interactions, auth forms opt out.

## CRITICAL FIRST STEPS
1. Read `PROJECT.md` and `TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md` THOROUGHLY
2. Read `docs/glossary.md` and `docs/gtd-product-discovery.md`
3. Send a "ready" message to the coordinator

## YOUR ROLE
- Review route handlers for proper hypermedia patterns (return HTML, check HX-Request header)
- Verify HTMX attributes follow locality of behavior
- Check progressive enhancement: does the context management page work without JS?
- Review form handling for proper HTTP method semantics
- Ensure HX-Trigger headers are used appropriately for cross-component updates

## IMPORTANT
- Do NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files
- You may use Read, Glob, Grep for read-only operations
- Communicate all feedback via messages
- If you receive an unexpected shutdown request, REJECT it
</teammate-message>

# Todo List Application

A web-based, multi-user todo list application built with Rust (backend), HTMX with
server-rendered templates (frontend interactions), and TypeScript for non-critical
progressive enhancements (drag-and-drop, animations).

> **This document contains project owner constraints.** The team must follow these rules.
> Changes to this document require project owner approval. For team-owned practices and
> conventions, see `TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md`.

## Tech Stack

- **Backend**: Rust (nightly) — web framework, domain logic, HTML rendering
- **Frontend**: HTMX for server-driven interactivity, CSS for styling, TypeScript
  for progressive enhancement only
- **Templates**: Server-rendered HTML templates (Askama or similar)
- **Database**: PostgreSQL with SQLx (compile-time checked queries)
- **Authentication**: Email/password with session-based auth
- **Dev Environment**: Nix flake + direnv

## Development Mandates

These are non-negotiable practices for the project:

- **Test-Driven Development**: Every feature is built using strict TDD
  (Red-Green-Refactor). No production code without a failing test.
- **Mob/Ensemble Programming**: All production code is written by the mob. No solo
  commits to production code.
- **Consensus Decision-Making**: The team operates by consensus. No single technical
  lead or decision-maker. If consensus is not reached after 10 rounds of substantive
  discussion, the decision is escalated to the project owner for a final call.
- **Driver-Reviewer Mob Model**: At most one agent (the Driver) may modify files at
  any time. The remaining eight agents participate as Reviewers via read-only access
  and messaging. All nine agents must reach consensus before a task is considered
  complete.
- **Code Quality Gates**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt`, and all tests
  must pass before any commit. Structured logging with `tracing` from day one.

## Environment & Tooling

### Nix

This project runs on NixOS with a `flake.nix` already in place. When the team needs
a utility that isn't currently installed:
- Use `nix shell nixpkgs#<package> --command <cmd>` for one-off commands
- For tools used regularly, add them to `buildInputs` in the devshell in `flake.nix`

### Agent Skills

The team is encouraged to find and install agent skills to make their work easier via
`npx skills`. The `find-skill` skill is already installed. When adding skills:
- Add them for **this project only** (not globally)
- Use support for **generic agent locations** (Codex, OpenCode, etc.) **and** Claude Code

### Custom Skills

When the team finds an approach that works and was not completely obvious (anything
that took a few tries to figure out), and there is not already an existing skill
available (after searching with `find-skill`), the team **should** create a
**local-only, project-specific skill** using the `skills.sh` format. This ensures
hard-won knowledge is captured and reusable.

### External Services (Docker)

If the team needs any external services running (e.g., a PostgreSQL database server),
use a `docker-compose.yml` file and Docker to run them. Do **not** run services
directly on the host machine. This keeps the dev environment reproducible and
self-contained.

## MVP Scope (Completed)

The original MVP (simple todo list) has been completed. All features below are shipped
and working:

- User registration (email/password)
- User login/logout
- View personal todo list
- Add a todo item
- Complete a todo item (toggle)
- Delete a todo item
- Edit a todo item title

## V1 Scope: Getting Things Done (GTD) System

The product is evolving from a simple todo list into a full GTD (Getting Things Done)
system. See `docs/gtd-product-discovery.md` for the complete product discovery document.

### Must Have
- **Inbox capture**: Quick capture of anything on the user's mind, minimal friction
- **Clarify workflow**: Process inbox items through GTD decision tree (actionable?
  next action? project? delegate? defer? trash?)
- **Next Actions list with contexts**: Actions organized by context (@computer, @home,
  @errands, etc.) so users work from context-appropriate lists
- **Projects list**: Track multi-step outcomes; each project has at least one next action;
  stalled projects are flagged
- **Waiting For list**: Track delegated/blocked items with who/what and date
- **Someday/Maybe list**: Park ideas without cluttering action lists
- **Weekly Review guided flow**: Three-phase review (Get Clear, Get Current, Get Creative)
  to keep the system trustworthy
- **GTD navigation and dashboard**: Navigate between all GTD lists; dashboard shows
  inbox count, stalled projects, action counts
- **Complete and delete**: Mark actions done, remove irrelevant items across all lists

### Should Have
- Project support materials (notes attached to projects)
- Move items between lists (reclassify as understanding changes)

### Could Have
- Review scheduling (remind when weekly review is due)
- Bulk inbox processing

### Out of Scope (Future)
- Calendar / date-specific items (requires date picker, time zones, recurring events)
- Reference file storage (needs file upload, search, storage infrastructure)
- Horizons of Focus / goals / vision (higher-level planning)
- Natural language input ("call Bob tomorrow")
- Email-to-inbox capture (requires email infrastructure)
- Mobile native app (responsive web is sufficient)
- Sharing / delegation to other users (multi-user collaboration)
- Tags beyond contexts
- Search and filter
- Dark mode
- Drag-and-drop reordering
- Recurring items
- Social login (OAuth)

1→# Domain Glossary
     2→
     3→Maps business terms to Rust types. This is the ubiquitous language of our GTD (Getting
     4→Things Done) application. Code should read like these definitions -- if the business
     5→says "capture to inbox," the code says `capture_to_inbox()`.
     6→
     7→Maintained by the mob. Changes reviewed by the domain architect (Scott Wlaschin).
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Core Domain Types
    12→
    13→| Domain Term | Rust Type | Module | Notes |
    14→|-------------|-----------|--------|-------|
    15→| User | `User` | `domain` | An authenticated user with valid credentials |
    16→| User ID | `UserId(Uuid)` | `domain` | Newtype wrapper; uniquely identifies a user |
    17→| Email address | `ValidatedEmail` | `domain` | Constructed via `ValidatedEmail::parse()`; validated format, normalized to lowercase (case-insensitive per US-2) |
    18→| Password | (not stored as domain type) | -- | Hashed with Argon2 at the boundary; the domain never sees raw passwords |
    19→| Todo item | `TodoItem` (enum) | `domain` | Sum type: `Pending` or `Completed` variant. No boolean flags. |
    20→| Todo item ID | `TodoItemId(Uuid)` | `domain` | Newtype wrapper; uniquely identifies a todo item |
    21→| Todo title | `TodoTitle` | `domain` | Non-empty, max 300 chars (per US-5), trimmed; constructed via `TodoTitle::parse()` |
    22→| Pending todo | `TodoItem::Pending { ... }` | `domain` | A todo that has not been completed; has title and created_at |
    23→| Completed todo | `TodoItem::Completed { ... }` | `domain` | A todo that has been completed; has title, created_at, and completed_at |
    24→
    25→## Domain Actions (Ubiquitous Language)
    26→
    27→| Business Action | Rust Function / Method | Notes |
    28→|-----------------|----------------------|-------|
    29→| Register a user | `register_user()` | Service: creates user with validated email and hashed password |
    30→| Log in | `authenticate_user()` | Service: verifies credentials; route stores user_id in session |
    31→| Log out | `post_logout()` | Route: flushes session, redirects to login |
    32→| Add a todo | `add_todo()` | Service: parses title, creates `TodoItem::Pending`, persists |
    33→| View todos | `get_todos()` | Service: fetches all todos for authenticated user |
    34→| Toggle completion | `toggle_todo_completion()` | Service: looks up todo, verifies ownership, toggles state |
    35→| Complete a todo | `TodoItem::complete()` | Domain method: Pending -> Completed; records completion time |
    36→| Uncomplete a todo | `TodoItem::uncomplete()` | Domain method: Completed -> Pending; drops completion time |
    37→| Delete a todo | `delete_todo()` | Service: verifies ownership, permanently removes from DB |
    38→| Edit a todo title | `update_todo_title()` | Service: verifies ownership, parses new title, persists |
    39→
    40→## Domain Errors
    41→
    42→| Error Condition | Rust Type | Notes |
    43→|-----------------|-----------|-------|
    44→| Title is empty | `TodoTitleError::Empty` | Todo title cannot be blank |
    45→| Title too long | `TodoTitleError::TooLong { max, actual }` | Exceeds maximum allowed length |
    46→| Todo not found (toggle) | `ToggleTodoError::NotFound` | Referenced todo does not exist |
    47→| Not authorized (toggle) | `ToggleTodoError::Unauthorized` | User does not own the todo |
    48→| Todo not found (delete) | `DeleteTodoError::NotFound` | Referenced todo does not exist |
    49→| Not authorized (delete) | `DeleteTodoError::Unauthorized` | User does not own the todo |
    50→| Email invalid | `RegistrationError::InvalidEmail(EmailValidationError)` | Email fails format validation |
    51→| Duplicate email | `RegistrationError::DuplicateEmail` | Registration attempted with existing email |
    52→| Invalid credentials | `AuthenticationError::InvalidCredentials` | Login failed (generic -- no info leak) |
    53→| Invalid title (add) | `AddTodoError::InvalidTitle(TodoTitleError)` | Title validation failure in add_todo service |
    54→| Todo not found (edit) | `UpdateTitleError::NotFound` | Referenced todo does not exist |
    55→| Not authorized (edit) | `UpdateTitleError::Unauthorized` | User does not own the todo |
    56→| Invalid title (edit) | `UpdateTitleError::InvalidTitle(TodoTitleError)` | Title validation failure in update_todo_title service |
    57→
    58→## Error Copy Convention
    59→
    60→Domain error messages (`#[error("...")]`) describe **what invariant was violated** -- they
    61→are written for developers and logs. User-facing error messages are **instructions** that
    62→tell the user what to fix -- they are written for humans scanning a form.
    63→
    64→The route/template layer translates domain errors into user-facing copy. Templates never
    65→call `error.to_string()` on a domain error.
    66→
    67→| Domain Error | User-Facing Copy |
    68→|-------------|-----------------|
    69→| `EmailValidationError::Empty` | "Enter your email address" |
    70→| `EmailValidationError::MissingAtSymbol` | "Enter a valid email address, like name@example.com" |
    71→| `EmailValidationError::TooLong` | "That email address is too long" |
    72→| `PasswordError::Empty` | "Enter a password" |
    73→| `PasswordError::TooShort` | "Your password needs at least 8 characters" |
    74→| `PasswordError::TooLong` | "That password is too long" |
    75→| `RegistrationError::DuplicateEmail` | "Unable to create account. If you already have an account, try signing in." |
    76→| `AuthenticationError::InvalidCredentials` | "That email or password didn't work. Try again." |
    77→| `TodoTitleError::Empty` (add) | (silently ignored per US-5 -- empty submissions are not errors) |
    78→| `TodoTitleError::Empty` (edit) | "Title cannot be empty" |
    79→| `TodoTitleError::TooLong` | "That title is too long (max 300 characters)" |
    80→
    81→*Copy reviewed by Steve Krug. Update this table when adding new error types.*
    82→
    83→---
    84→
    85→## Type Design Principles
    86→
    87→These principles govern how we model the domain:
    88→
    89→1. **Parse, don't validate**: `TodoTitle::parse(input) -> Result<TodoTitle, TodoError>`
    90→   not `fn is_valid_title(input: &str) -> bool`. Parsing produces a typed value that
    91→   carries proof of validity. Validate once at the boundary; never re-validate inside.
    92→
    93→2. **Make illegal states unrepresentable**: A `TodoItem` is either `Pending` or
    94→   `Completed` -- never both, never neither. The enum enforces this at compile time.
    95→   No boolean `is_completed` flag with an `Option<completed_at>` that could be
    96→   inconsistent.
    97→
    98→3. **Newtypes for everything**: `UserId` is not a bare `Uuid`. `ValidatedEmail` is
    99→   not a bare `String`. The type system prevents mixing them up. These are zero-cost
   100→   abstractions in Rust.
   101→
   102→4. **Pure domain core**: Everything in `src/domain/` is pure functions and types. No
   103→   database imports, no HTTP types, no I/O. If you need a mock to test domain logic,
   104→   I/O has leaked into the domain.
   105→
   106→5. **Workflows as type-safe pipelines**:
   107→   ```
   108→   UnvalidatedInput -> ValidatedInput -> DomainResult
   109→   ```
   110→   Each step's output type is the next step's input. You cannot skip validation.
   111→
   112→## State Machine: TodoItem
   113→
   114→```
   115→                 add_todo()
   116→  [nothing] ────────────────> Pending
   117→                                 │  ^
   118→               item.complete()   │  │  item.uncomplete()
   119→                                 │  │
   120→                                 v  │
   121→                             Completed
   122→```
   123→
   124→Both `Pending` and `Completed` variants can be deleted (`delete_todo()`).
   125→The `update_todo_title()` action applies to both states.
   126→Completion is **reversible** -- users can toggle between Pending and Completed (US-6).
   127→
   128→---
   129→
   130→## GTD Domain Types (V1 -- Approved)
   131→
   132→*These types will replace the simple TodoItem model as we evolve to GTD. Approved by
   133→team consensus. Final type signatures subject to domain architect guidance during
   134→implementation.*
   135→
   136→### Core GTD Concepts
   137→
   138→| Domain Term | Proposed Rust Type | Notes |
   139→|-------------|-------------------|-------|
   140→| Inbox item | `InboxItem` | Raw, unclarified capture. Has title and created_at. No context, no project link. |
   141→| Next action | `NextAction` (enum: Active, Completed) | A concrete, physical action ready to do. Has context and optional project link. |
   142→| Project | `Project` (enum: Active, Completed, Dropped) | An outcome requiring 2+ actions. Has title and linked next actions. |
   143→| Context | `Context` | Where/how an action can be performed. User-defined. Defaults: @computer, @home, @errands, @phone, @anywhere |
   144→| Waiting For item | `WaitingForItem` | Delegated/blocked item. Has title, waiting_on (text), and date added. |
   145→| Someday/Maybe item | `SomedayMaybeItem` | Parked idea. Has title and created_at. Not committed to. |
   146→| Item title | `ItemTitle` | Replaces `TodoTitle`. Non-empty, max 300 chars, trimmed. Same validation. |
   147→| Context name | `ContextName` | Non-empty, max 50 chars, trimmed. Validated at construction. |
   148→
   149→### GTD Actions (Ubiquitous Language)
   150→
   151→| Business Action | Proposed Rust Function / Method | Notes |
   152→|-----------------|-------------------------------|-------|
   153→| Capture to inbox | `capture_to_inbox()` | Creates InboxItem from raw text |
   154→| Clarify as next action | `clarify_as_next_action()` | InboxItem -> NextAction (requires context) |
   155→| Clarify as project | `clarify_as_project()` | InboxItem -> Project + first NextAction |
   156→| Delegate | `delegate()` | InboxItem or NextAction -> WaitingForItem |
   157→| Defer | `defer()` | InboxItem or NextAction -> SomedayMaybeItem |
   158→| Trash | (delete) | InboxItem is permanently removed |
   159→| Complete action | `NextAction::complete()` | Active -> Completed; records completion time |
   160→| Complete project | `Project::complete()` | Active -> Completed; records completion time |
   161→| Drop project | `Project::drop()` | Active -> Dropped; project abandoned |
   162→| Activate someday/maybe | `activate()` | SomedayMaybeItem -> InboxItem (for re-clarification) |
   163→| Resolve waiting for | `resolve()` | WaitingForItem -> completed or moved to inbox |
   164→| Add context | `add_context()` | Creates a new user-defined context |
   165→| Start weekly review | `start_review()` | Initiates the three-phase review flow |
   166→
   167→### GTD State Machines
   168→
   169→**InboxItem Lifecycle:**
   170→```
   171→                capture()
   172→  [nothing] ──────────────> InboxItem
   173→                                │
   174→                      clarify() │
   175→              ┌────────┬────────┼────────┬────────┐
   176→              v        v        v        v        v
   177→         NextAction  Project  WaitingFor  Someday  [trash]
   178→```
   179→
   180→**NextAction Lifecycle:**
   181→```
   182→                  clarify_as_next_action()
   183→  InboxItem ─────────────────────────────> NextAction (Active)
   184→                                               │
   185→                                   complete()  │
   186→                                               v
   187→                                         NextAction (Completed)
   188→```
   189→
   190→**Project Lifecycle:**
   191→```
   192→                    clarify_as_project()
   193→  InboxItem ──────────────────────────> Project (Active)
   194→                                           │  │
   195→                              complete()   │  │  drop()
   196→                                           v  v
   197→                                    Completed / Dropped
   198→```
   199→

# Domain Glossary

Maps business terms to Rust types. This is the ubiquitous language of our GTD (Getting
Things Done) application. Code should read like these definitions -- if the business
says "capture to inbox," the code says `capture_to_inbox()`.

Maintained by the mob. Changes reviewed by the domain architect (Scott Wlaschin).

---

## Core Domain Types

| Domain Term | Rust Type | Module | Notes |
|-------------|-----------|--------|-------|
| User | `User` | `domain` | An authenticated user with valid credentials |
| User ID | `UserId(Uuid)` | `domain` | Newtype wrapper; uniquely identifies a user |
| Email address | `ValidatedEmail` | `domain` | Constructed via `ValidatedEmail::parse()`; validated format, normalized to lowercase (case-insensitive per US-2) |
| Password | (not stored as domain type) | -- | Hashed with Argon2 at the boundary; the domain never sees raw passwords |
| Todo item | `TodoItem` (enum) | `domain` | Sum type: `Pending` or `Completed` variant. No boolean flags. |
| Todo item ID | `TodoItemId(Uuid)` | `domain` | Newtype wrapper; uniquely identifies a todo item |
| Todo title | `TodoTitle` | `domain` | Non-empty, max 300 chars (per US-5), trimmed; constructed via `TodoTitle::parse()` |
| Pending todo | `TodoItem::Pending { ... }` | `domain` | A todo that has not been completed; has title and created_at |
| Completed todo | `TodoItem::Completed { ... }` | `domain` | A todo that has been completed; has title, created_at, and completed_at |

## Domain Actions (Ubiquitous Language)

| Business Action | Rust Function / Method | Notes |
|-----------------|----------------------|-------|
| Register a user | `register_user()` | Service: creates user with validated email and hashed password |
| Log in | `authenticate_user()` | Service: verifies credentials; route stores user_id in session |
| Log out | `post_logout()` | Route: flushes session, redirects to login |
| Add a todo | `add_todo()` | Service: parses title, creates `TodoItem::Pending`, persists |
| View todos | `get_todos()` | Service: fetches all todos for authenticated user |
| Toggle completion | `toggle_todo_completion()` | Service: looks up todo, verifies ownership, toggles state |
| Complete a todo | `TodoItem::complete()` | Domain method: Pending -> Completed; records completion time |
| Uncomplete a todo | `TodoItem::uncomplete()` | Domain method: Completed -> Pending; drops completion time |
| Delete a todo | `delete_todo()` | Service: verifies ownership, permanently removes from DB |
| Edit a todo title | `update_todo_title()` | Service: verifies ownership, parses new title, persists |

## Domain Errors

| Error Condition | Rust Type | Notes |
|-----------------|-----------|-------|
| Title is empty | `TodoTitleError::Empty` | Todo title cannot be blank |
| Title too long | `TodoTitleError::TooLong { max, actual }` | Exceeds maximum allowed length |
| Todo not found (toggle) | `ToggleTodoError::NotFound` | Referenced todo does not exist |
| Not authorized (toggle) | `ToggleTodoError::Unauthorized` | User does not own the todo |
| Todo not found (delete) | `DeleteTodoError::NotFound` | Referenced todo does not exist |
| Not authorized (delete) | `DeleteTodoError::Unauthorized` | User does not own the todo |
| Email invalid | `RegistrationError::InvalidEmail(EmailValidationError)` | Email fails format validation |
| Duplicate email | `RegistrationError::DuplicateEmail` | Registration attempted with existing email |
| Invalid credentials | `AuthenticationError::InvalidCredentials` | Login failed (generic -- no info leak) |
| Invalid title (add) | `AddTodoError::InvalidTitle(TodoTitleError)` | Title validation failure in add_todo service |
| Todo not found (edit) | `UpdateTitleError::NotFound` | Referenced todo does not exist |
| Not authorized (edit) | `UpdateTitleError::Unauthorized` | User does not own the todo |
| Invalid title (edit) | `UpdateTitleError::InvalidTitle(TodoTitleError)` | Title validation failure in update_todo_title service |

## Error Copy Convention

Domain error messages (`#[error("...")]`) describe **what invariant was violated** -- they
are written for developers and logs. User-facing error messages are **instructions** that
tell the user what to fix -- they are written for humans scanning a form.

The route/template layer translates domain errors into user-facing copy. Templates never
call `error.to_string()` on a domain error.

| Domain Error | User-Facing Copy |
|-------------|-----------------|
| `EmailValidationError::Empty` | "Enter your email address" |
| `EmailValidationError::MissingAtSymbol` | "Enter a valid email address, like name@example.com" |
| `EmailValidationError::TooLong` | "That email address is too long" |
| `PasswordError::Empty` | "Enter a password" |
| `PasswordError::TooShort` | "Your password needs at least 8 characters" |
| `PasswordError::TooLong` | "That password is too long" |
| `RegistrationError::DuplicateEmail` | "Unable to create account. If you already have an account, try signing in." |
| `AuthenticationError::InvalidCredentials` | "That email or password didn't work. Try again." |
| `TodoTitleError::Empty` (add) | (silently ignored per US-5 -- empty submissions are not errors) |
| `TodoTitleError::Empty` (edit) | "Title cannot be empty" |
| `TodoTitleError::TooLong` | "That title is too long (max 300 characters)" |

*Copy reviewed by Steve Krug. Update this table when adding new error types.*

---

## Type Design Principles

These principles govern how we model the domain:

1. **Parse, don't validate**: `TodoTitle::parse(input) -> Result<TodoTitle, TodoError>`
   not `fn is_valid_title(input: &str) -> bool`. Parsing produces a typed value that
   carries proof of validity. Validate once at the boundary; never re-validate inside.

2. **Make illegal states unrepresentable**: A `TodoItem` is either `Pending` or
   `Completed` -- never both, never neither. The enum enforces this at compile time.
   No boolean `is_completed` flag with an `Option<completed_at>` that could be
   inconsistent.

3. **Newtypes for everything**: `UserId` is not a bare `Uuid`. `ValidatedEmail` is
   not a bare `String`. The type system prevents mixing them up. These are zero-cost
   abstractions in Rust.

4. **Pure domain core**: Everything in `src/domain/` is pure functions and types. No
   database imports, no HTTP types, no I/O. If you need a mock to test domain logic,
   I/O has leaked into the domain.

5. **Workflows as type-safe pipelines**:
   ```
   UnvalidatedInput -> ValidatedInput -> DomainResult
   ```
   Each step's output type is the next step's input. You cannot skip validation.

## State Machine: TodoItem

```
                 add_todo()
  [nothing] ────────────────> Pending
                                 │  ^
               item.complete()   │  │  item.uncomplete()
                                 │  │
                                 v  │
                             Completed
```

Both `Pending` and `Completed` variants can be deleted (`delete_todo()`).
The `update_todo_title()` action applies to both states.
Completion is **reversible** -- users can toggle between Pending and Completed (US-6).

---

## GTD Domain Types (V1 -- Approved)

*These types will replace the simple TodoItem model as we evolve to GTD. Approved by
team consensus. Final type signatures subject to domain architect guidance during
implementation.*

### Core GTD Concepts

| Domain Term | Proposed Rust Type | Notes |
|-------------|-------------------|-------|
| Inbox item | `InboxItem` | Raw, unclarified capture. Has title and created_at. No context, no project link. |
| Next action | `NextAction` (enum: Active, Completed) | A concrete, physical action ready to do. Has context and optional project link. |
| Project | `Project` (enum: Active, Completed, Dropped) | An outcome requiring 2+ actions. Has title and linked next actions. |
| Context | `Context` | Where/how an action can be performed. User-defined. Defaults: @computer, @home, @errands, @phone, @anywhere |
| Waiting For item | `WaitingForItem` | Delegated/blocked item. Has title, waiting_on (text), and date added. |
| Someday/Maybe item | `SomedayMaybeItem` | Parked idea. Has title and created_at. Not committed to. |
| Item title | `ItemTitle` | Replaces `TodoTitle`. Non-empty, max 300 chars, trimmed. Same validation. |
| Context name | `ContextName` | Non-empty, max 50 chars, trimmed. Validated at construction. |

### GTD Actions (Ubiquitous Language)

| Business Action | Proposed Rust Function / Method | Notes |
|-----------------|-------------------------------|-------|
| Capture to inbox | `capture_to_inbox()` | Creates InboxItem from raw text |
| Clarify as next action | `clarify_as_next_action()` | InboxItem -> NextAction (requires context) |
| Clarify as project | `clarify_as_project()` | InboxItem -> Project + first NextAction |
| Delegate | `delegate()` | InboxItem or NextAction -> WaitingForItem |
| Defer | `defer()` | InboxItem or NextAction -> SomedayMaybeItem |
| Trash | (delete) | InboxItem is permanently removed |
| Complete action | `NextAction::complete()` | Active -> Completed; records completion time |
| Complete project | `Project::complete()` | Active -> Completed; records completion time |
| Drop project | `Project::drop()` | Active -> Dropped; project abandoned |
| Activate someday/maybe | `activate()` | SomedayMaybeItem -> InboxItem (for re-clarification) |
| Resolve waiting for | `resolve()` | WaitingForItem -> completed or moved to inbox |
| Add context | `add_context()` | Creates a new user-defined context |
| Start weekly review | `start_review()` | Initiates the three-phase review flow |

### GTD State Machines

**InboxItem Lifecycle:**
```
                capture()
  [nothing] ──────────────> InboxItem
                                │
                      clarify() │
              ┌────────┬────────┼────────┬────────┐
              v        v        v        v        v
         NextAction  Project  WaitingFor  Someday  [trash]
```

**NextAction Lifecycle:**
```
                  clarify_as_next_action()
  InboxItem ─────────────────────────────> NextAction (Active)
                                               │
                                   complete()  │
                                               v
                                         NextAction (Completed)
```

**Project Lifecycle:**
```
                    clarify_as_project()
  InboxItem ──────────────────────────> Project (Active)
                                           │  │
                              complete()   │  │  drop()
                                           v  v
                                    Completed / Dropped
```

   272→  - *Structural*: HTML validation for semantic correctness

   277→
   278→Proposed starting dependencies (open to mob discussion):
   279→
   280→| Crate | Purpose |
   281→|-------|---------|
   282→| `axum` | Web framework |
   283→| `tokio` (full features) | Async runtime |
   284→| `sqlx` (postgres, migrate, runtime-tokio-rustls) | Database |

   290→| `argon2` | Password hashing |
   291→| `tower-sessions` + `tower-sessions-sqlx-store` | Session management |
   292→| `uuid` | Identifiers |
   293→
   294→Note: Input validation will be handled through custom newtypes with validation in
   295→constructors (Scott's approach), not a validation framework. The types carry the
   296→proof of validity.
   297→
   298→### HTML / HTMX Conventions
   299→
   300→**The Hypermedia Contract:**
   301→- **Return HTML, not JSON.** Every UI endpoint returns HTML. No JSON API layer.
   302→- **Semantic HTML first**: `<button>` for actions, `<a>` for navigation, `<form>` for
   303→  data submission, `<ul>`/`<ol>` for lists. Never `<div onclick>`.
   304→- **Form accessibility**: Every input has a visible `<label>` (never placeholder-only).
   305→  Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby` and announced
   306→  via `aria-live="polite"` regions. Use `aria-live="assertive"` only for critical errors.
   307→- **Locality of behavior**: Every HTMX-enhanced element is self-describing. You
   308→  understand what it does by looking at it alone.
   309→- **No client-side state**: The DOM is the state, the server is the source of truth.
   310→  No client-side data stores. The only acceptable client-side state is transient UI
   311→  state for progressive enhancement (e.g., drag position).
   312→
   313→**Server endpoint conventions:**
   314→- **Full page requests** (no `HX-Request` header): Return complete HTML page with
   315→  layout, nav, landmarks.
   316→- **HTMX requests** (with `HX-Request` header): Return just the HTML fragment for
   317→  the swap.
   318→- **`HX-Trigger` response header** for cross-component updates (e.g., updating a
   319→  todo count after deletion).
   320→- **Same URL, same handler**: One handler checks the `HX-Request` header to decide
   321→  whether to return a full page or a fragment. No separate `/api/` routes.
   322→- **HTTP methods used semantically**: GET = read, POST = create, PUT/PATCH = update,
   323→  DELETE = delete.
   324→- **Status codes**: 200 for success with HTML, 422 for validation errors with error
   325→  HTML, 200 with empty body for deletion (allows HTMX swap to remove element).
   326→
   327→**HTMX Architecture Strategy:**
   328→- `hx-boost="true"` on `<body>` — universal navigation enhancement. All `<a>` and
   329→  `<form>` elements automatically use AJAX with history management. No per-link
   330→  attributes needed for standard navigation.
   331→- **Explicit attributes for in-page interactions**: Use `hx-post`, `hx-target`,
   332→  `hx-swap` for operations that update part of the page without navigation (e.g.,
   333→  adding a todo item, deleting an inbox item).
   334→- **Auth forms opt out** with `hx-boost="false"` — login/logout/register forms need
   335→  full page redirects for session cookie handling.
   336→- **Server renders full pages; boost handles swap transparently.** Handlers check the
   337→  `HX-Request` header to decide whether to return a full page or a fragment.
   338→
   339→**Progressive enhancement (non-negotiable):**
   340→- The app MUST work without JavaScript. Forms submit, links navigate, content is
   341→  readable with HTML alone.
   342→- HTMX enhances the experience (partial updates, no full page reloads).
   343→- TypeScript is only for things neither HTML nor HTMX can do.
   344→
   345→**HTMX Focus Management Protocol:**
   346→After every HTMX swap:
   347→- If an element was **removed**: Move focus to the logical next element.
   348→- If content was **updated in place**: Keep focus where it was.
   349→- Use `aria-live="polite"` regions to announce state changes to screen readers.
   350→- Test every HTMX interaction with Tab key before considering it done.
   351→
   352→**Page structure (every page):**
   353→- Skip link to main content (first focusable element)
   354→- Landmark regions: `<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`, `<footer>`
   355→- Logical heading hierarchy: one `<h1>` per page, no skipped levels
   356→- Descriptive `<title>` element
   357→
   358→### CSS Conventions
   359→
   360→**Progressive enhancement layers:**
   361→1. **Layer 1 (HTML)**: Functional and readable with zero CSS.
   362→2. **Layer 2 (CSS)**: Beautiful -- layout, typography, color, transitions, focus styles.
   363→3. **Layer 3 (TypeScript)**: Delightful -- drag-and-drop, advanced keyboard shortcuts.
   364→
   365→**CSS organization with cascade layers:**
   366→```css
   367→@layer reset, tokens, base, layout, components, utilities;
   368→```
   369→- **reset**: Normalize/reset styles
   370→- **tokens**: Custom properties (design tokens)
   371→- **base**: Element-level styles (body, headings, links, forms)
   372→- **layout**: Page-level layout (CSS Grid)
   373→- **components**: Component-specific styles (todo-item, auth-form)
   374→- **utilities**: Helper classes (visually-hidden, etc.)
   375→- No `!important` unless in the utilities layer for intentional overrides.
   376→- CSS nesting is fine for component-scoped styles but avoid nesting deeper than
   377→  2 levels.
   378→
   379→**Design tokens (CSS custom properties on `:root`):**
   380→- **Spacing scale** (4px base): 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64, 96 -- encoded as
   381→  `--space-xs` through `--space-3xl`
   382→- **Color palette**: oklch color space for perceptual uniformity. One primary color,
   383→  neutral gray palette (50-900), semantic colors (danger, success, warning). Most UI
   384→  is grayscale; color is for emphasis and interaction.
   385→- **Typography**: System font stack (`system-ui, sans-serif`). Type scale from xs to
   386→  3xl with defined line-height. Tighter for headings, looser for body.
   387→- **Shadows**: Three levels (sm, md, lg). Never combine shadows and borders on the
   388→  same element.
   389→- **Border radius**: Three sizes (sm, md, lg).
   390→- **All values reference tokens -- no hardcoded hex values or pixel values in
   391→  component styles.**
   392→
   393→**Design tokens are a prerequisite**: The token system (spacing, colors, typography,
   394→shadows, radii) must be established and agreed upon before any feature UI work begins.
   395→This is foundational -- without it, inconsistent one-off values accumulate from day one.
   396→
   397→**Design token governance**: Adding a new color, spacing value, or typography level
   398→goes through the mob (Steve Schoger approves design token changes).
   399→
   400→**Design token flow**: Steve Schoger defines the visual design -> Lea encodes as CSS
   401→custom properties -> Components reference only tokens -> Themes/dark mode reassign
   402→properties. No build step. No JSON-to-CSS pipeline.
   403→
   404→**Modern CSS features:**
   405→- `:has()` for parent-based styling (e.g., `li:has(input:checked)` for completed todos)
   406→- Container queries where components need to respond to container size
   407→- Logical properties (`margin-inline`, `padding-block`) for future RTL support
   408→- `clamp()` for fluid typography
   409→- View Transitions API for smooth HTMX swap animations
   410→- `@supports` for graceful degradation of newer features
   411→
   412→**Accessibility in CSS:**
   413→- Custom `:focus-visible` styles with at least 3:1 contrast. Never `outline: none`
   414→  without a replacement.
   415→- `prefers-reduced-motion`: All animations/transitions wrapped in a motion query.
   416→  Reduced or no motion as the alternative.
   417→- `prefers-color-scheme`: Supported if/when the team decides to add dark mode.
   418→- Color is never the only state indicator.
   419→
   420→**Responsive design**: Mobile-first. Default styles target the smallest viewport;
   421→enhancements added via `min-width` media queries. Breakpoints: mobile (default),
   422→desktop (~1024px). A todo list should work beautifully on a phone.
   423→
   424→**Browser targets**: Last 2 versions of evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari,
   425→Edge). No IE11. `@supports` for graceful degradation. No polyfills unless necessary.
   426→
   427→### TypeScript Conventions
   428→- **No framework**: Vanilla TypeScript with DOM APIs.
   429→- **Progressive enhancement pattern**: Feature works without JS, TypeScript enhances.
   430→- **Event delegation**: Listeners on parent containers, not individual elements.
   431→- **State via data attributes**: TypeScript toggles `data-*` attributes or classes;
   432→  CSS handles all visual changes based on those.
   433→- **No CSS-in-JS**: Styles live in CSS files. TypeScript never sets inline styles
   434→  (except computed values like drag position).
   435→- **Thin layer**: If a TypeScript file is getting large, we are probably doing
   436→  something CSS or HTMX could handle.
   437→
   438→### Copy Standards
   439→- **Labels**: 1-2 words when possible.
   440→- **Error messages**: Plain language. Say what went wrong, suggest what to do next.
   441→  "That email isn't registered -- want to create an account?" not "Error: User not found."
   442→- **Button labels**: Describe the action ("Add todo", "Sign in"), not vague labels
   443→  ("Submit", "OK").
   444→- **No happy talk**: Every word earns its place or gets cut. No introductory paragraphs
   445→  that say nothing useful.
   446→- **Design for scanning, not reading**: Users don't read pages, they scan. Clear
   447→  headings, short text, prominent calls to action, no walls of text.
   448→- **Cut the text, then cut it again**: If you wrote a paragraph, ask "can I say this
   449→  in half the words?"
   450→
   451→### Domain Language and Modeling Conventions
   452→- Code uses the ubiquitous language of the domain. "Complete a task" ->
   453→  `complete_task()`, not `update_status()`.
   454→- Domain types are explicit -- no primitive obsession. Every domain concept gets its
   455→  own type, even if it is "just a string" or "just a UUID" underneath.
   456→- Newtypes are constructed only through validated factory methods (the `parse`
   457→  pattern). You cannot create a `TodoTitle` from an arbitrary string without going
   458→  through validation.
   459→- Every `is_*` boolean flag is a candidate for an enum. Ask: "what states does this
   460→  represent?" and model them explicitly.
   461→- Maintain a domain glossary in `docs/glossary.md` mapping business terms to types:
   462→
   463→  | Domain Term | Rust Type | Notes |
   464→  |-------------|-----------|-------|
   465→  | User | `User` | Authenticated user with valid credentials |
   466→  | Email | `ValidatedEmail` | Constructed via `parse`, not `new` |
   467→  | Todo item | `TodoItem` (enum) | `Pending` or `Completed` variant |
   468→  | Todo title | `TodoTitle` | Non-empty, max length, validated at construction |
   469→
   470→### Observability
   471→
   472→**Structured logging with `tracing`:**
   473→- Request ID (UUID) attached to every request via middleware.
   474→- Span fields: `request_id`, `method`, `uri`, `status_code`.
   475→- Application-level spans for significant operations: `register_user`,
   476→  `authenticate`, `create_todo`, etc.
   477→- Use `#[tracing::instrument]` for service functions.
   478→- Structured fields on errors: what failed, why, relevant IDs.
   479→
   480→**Log level conventions:**
   481→| Level | When to use |
   482→|-------|-------------|
   483→| `ERROR` | Something is broken, needs human attention |
   484→| `WARN` | Unexpected but handled (e.g., invalid login attempt) |
   485→| `INFO` | Significant business events (user registered, todo created) |
   486→| `DEBUG` | Technical details useful for debugging |
   487→| `TRACE` | Very verbose (request/response bodies, SQL queries) |
   488→
   489→### Database Conventions
   490→- **Migrations**: SQLx migrations (`sqlx migrate`), numbered SQL files, version controlled.
   491→- **Compile-time checked queries**: `sqlx::query!` and `sqlx::query_as!` macros.
   492→- **Offline mode for CI**: `sqlx-data.json` generated via `cargo sqlx prepare`,
   493→  committed to version control.
   494→- **Connection pool**: `PgPool` shared via Axum state.
   495→- **UUIDs for primary keys**: `uuid` type in PostgreSQL, `Uuid` in Rust.
   496→- **Timestamps**: `timestamptz` in PostgreSQL, `chrono::DateTime<Utc>` or
   497→  `time::OffsetDateTime` in Rust.
   498→- **Naming conventions**: snake_case for tables and columns. Plural table names
   499→  (`users`, `todos`).
   500→
   501→### Configuration Management
   502→- Layered configuration: `base.yaml` (defaults) -> `local.yaml` (gitignored overrides)
   503→  -> environment variables (12-factor style).
   504→- **Fail fast**: Missing required configuration at startup panics with a clear message.
   505→  No silent defaults for critical settings (database URL, secret keys).
   506→- Separate configuration structs: `DatabaseSettings`, `ApplicationSettings`,
   507→  `AuthSettings`.
   508→
   509→### CI/CD Quality Gates
   510→
   511→Every commit must pass (in order):
   512→1. `cargo fmt --check`
   513→2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
   514→3. `cargo test` (unit + integration)
   515→4. `npx playwright test` (browser-based e2e)
   516→5. `cargo audit` (dependency vulnerabilities)
   517→
   518→### Atomic Green Step Pipeline
   519→
   520→*Every change follows this pipeline. No shortcuts, no skipped steps.*
   521→
   522→1. **Write code** — the smallest meaningful change.
   523→2. **`cargo fmt --check`** — fix formatting if needed.
   524→3. **`cargo clippy -- -D warnings`** — fix warnings if needed.
   525→4. **`cargo test`** — fix failures if needed.
   526→5. **`npx playwright test`** — fix e2e failures if needed.
   527→6. **Refactor** — look for duplication, unclear names, or structural improvements
   528→   before committing. The Refactor step is not optional.
   529→7. **Glossary check** — verify new domain types/fields match `docs/glossary.md`. If
   530→   deviating, update the glossary first (with domain architect approval).
   531→8. **`git commit`** — commit locally. **Do NOT push yet.**
   532→9. **All 9 agents review and reach consensus** — Driver and Reviewers confirm the change
   533→   is correct. If concerns are raised, the Driver fixes them, re-runs steps 2–7, and
   534→   re-seeks consensus.
   535→10. **Once 9/9 consensus → `git push`.**
   536→11. **Wait for CI green** — run `gh run list --limit 1` and WAIT for CI to complete
   537→    green before beginning the next change. Never have more than 1 pending CI run. If
   538→    CI fails, fix it before pushing anything else.
   539→12. **Mini-retrospective** (1-minute checkpoint) — Driver confirms: (a) Did we follow
   540→    the pipeline? (b) Was the commit atomic? (c) Any process improvements?
   541→13. **Only then begin the next change.**
   542→
   543→Additional rules:
   544→- **Consensus before push.** Code is reviewed locally before it reaches the remote. This
   545→  keeps the CI wait out of the feedback loop.
   546→- **If CI fails, fix it before writing new code.** A red CI is the team's top priority.
   547→  No new features, no new tests, no refactoring until CI is green again.
   548→- **One Driver at a time.** Only the designated Driver may modify files. Non-driver agents
   549→  MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files. Violation is treated as a
   550→  process failure.
   551→
   552→### Dependency Management
   553→
   554→Before adding a new crate:
   555→- Is it well-maintained? (Recent commits, responsive maintainers)
   556→- Is it widely used? (Downloads, reverse dependencies)
   557→- Compatible license? (MIT/Apache-2.0 preferred)
   558→- Can we avoid it? (Prefer stdlib or existing dependencies)
   559→
   560→### Documentation
   561→- **Code comments**: Only where "why" is not obvious from the code.
   562→- **ADRs**: For significant technical decisions. Format: context, decision,
   563→  consequences. Stored in `docs/adr/`.
   564→- **Domain glossary**: `docs/glossary.md` mapping business terms to code.
   565→- **README**: Practical -- how to set up, run tests, run the app.
   566→
   567→---
   568→
   569→## 4. Disagreement Resolution
   570→
   571→### In the Mob (Day-to-Day)
   572→1. State the disagreement clearly: "I think X, you think Y."
   573→2. Each side explains their reasoning briefly (< 2 minutes each).
   574→3. If possible, **try both and let the code decide** -- write a quick test or prototype.
   575→4. If one approach is simpler, prefer it (smallest viable decision).
   576→5. If still stuck after 5 minutes, the mob votes. Majority wins for day-to-day decisions.
   577→   The dissenter can say "I disagree but won't block."
   578→
   579→### For Significant Decisions (Consensus Protocol)
   580→1. Any team member raises the decision point with a proposal and reasoning.
   581→2. Each team member shares their perspective (concerns, alternatives, support).
   582→3. Discussion continues until:
   583→   - **Consensus**: Everyone agrees or consents ("I can live with this").
   584→   - **Compromise**: A synthesized solution addresses all concerns.
   585→   - **Stand-aside**: "I disagree but won't block" -- recorded and respected.
   586→4. If no consensus after **10 rounds** of substantive discussion, **escalate to the
   587→   project owner** with:
   588→   - Options considered
   589→   - Each member's position
   590→   - Trade-offs
   591→   - Team recommendation (if possible)
   592→
   593→### When to Involve the Project Owner
   594→- Consensus deadlock after 10 rounds
   595→- Any proposed change to MVP scope
   596→- Questions about target user or business context the team cannot answer
   597→- Major architectural pivots that significantly change the timeline
   598→- Brief status updates at milestones (keep the owner informed, don't go dark)
   599→
   600→### Principles
   601→- Disagree with the idea, not the person.
   602→- Once decided, **disagree and commit** -- no undermining, no "I told you so."
   603→- Revisit decisions when new information emerges (from tests, users, or production).
   604→- **Write it down**: Major decisions recorded as Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
   605→  in `docs/adr/`.
   606→
   607→---
   608→
   609→## 5. Architectural Principles
   610→
   611→1. **Simplicity first**: Choose the simplest approach that could work. Add complexity
   612→   only when tests or user feedback demand it. "Could this be simpler?" is always a
   613→   valid question.
   614→2. **Server-side state**: The server is the source of truth for both data and
   615→   presentation. The browser renders what the server sends. No client-side state
   616→   management.
   617→3. **Pure domain core**: Domain logic is pure functions and types with no I/O. Side
   618→   effects live at the boundaries. This makes the domain trivially testable.
   619→4. **Make illegal states unrepresentable**: Use the type system to prevent bugs at
   620→   compile time. Newtypes, enums, and validated types carry proof of correctness.
   621→5. **Production-ready from day one**: Observability, error handling, and structured
   622→   logging from the first line of code -- not bolted on later.
   623→6. **Accessibility is a quality, not a feature**: Every feature is accessible from the
   624→   moment it is built. Inaccessible is broken. ~15-20% of users have some form of
   625→   disability -- that is not an edge case.
   626→7. **Progressive enhancement**: HTML works -> CSS makes it beautiful -> JS makes it
   627→   delightful. Each layer enhances without being required by the layers above.
   628→8. **Conventions over cleverness**: Follow established patterns. A login form should
   629→   look like every other login form. Don't make the user think. Design for scanning,
   630→   not reading. Cut unnecessary words. When in doubt, simplify.
   631→9. **Continuous testing**: TDD keeps us honest. The compiler is our first test. We
   632→   never skip the red step. Tests read like behavior specifications.
   633→10. **Incremental design**: Let the design emerge from tests and feedback. Don't
   634→    over-design upfront. Make the code a little better before adding a feature (Tidy
   635→    First), but don't redesign the universe. Ship early, learn fast.
   636→11. **Walking skeleton first**: Our first milestone is the thinnest possible slice
   637→    through the entire stack -- a user can register, log in, see an empty todo list,
   638→    add one item, and log out. Everything else builds on top.
   639→12. **Validate at the boundary, trust the types**: Input validation happens once, at
   640→    the system boundary. It produces typed values that carry proof of validity.
   641→    The interior of the system trusts its own types.
   642→
   643→---
   644→
   645→## 6. Retrospective Cadence
   646→
   647→All team members are AI agents operating continuously. Time-based retros (weekly,
   648→bi-weekly) do not apply. Instead, retros are triggered by events.
   649→
   650→- **After each feature**: Retro immediately after completing a feature. What worked?
   651→  What didn't? What should we change?
   652→- **After significant incidents**: If a build breaks, a major refactor goes sideways,
   653→  or the team gets stuck for an extended period, retro after resolution.
   654→- **Format**: Simple Start/Stop/Continue.
   655→- **Action items**: Every retro produces at most 2-3 concrete action items. These are
   656→  tracked and checked at the next retro.
   657→- **Agreements are living**: These team agreements can be updated at any retro if the
   658→  team reaches consensus.
   659→- **Usability testing**: Aim for informal testing -- three users, 20 minutes each --
   660→  at natural breakpoints. Even watching one person use the app for 5 minutes reveals
   661→  more than a week of discussion.
   662→- **Post-CI checkpoint**: After each CI build (pass or fail), the Driver briefly
   663→  confirms: (a) Did we follow the pipeline? (b) Was the commit atomic? (c) Any
   664→  process improvements? This is a 1-minute checkpoint, not a full retro.
   665→- **Deferred items**: Non-blocking a11y, design, and UX items are tracked in
   666→  `docs/deferred-items.md` and reviewed at each retrospective.
   667→
   668→---
   669→
   670→## 7. Tooling & Repository
   671→
   672→### Source Control
   673→- **Repository**: Public GitHub repository under the `jwilger` account
   674→- **License**: MIT
   675→- **Branching**: Work on `main` for now. Feature branches if/when the team decides
   676→  they are needed.
   677→
   678→### CI/CD
   679→- **GitHub Actions**: CI runs on every push and pull request to `main`.
   680→- **Pipeline stages** (in order):
   681→  1. `cargo fmt --check`
   682→  2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
   683→  3. `cargo test`
   684→  4. `npx playwright test`
   685→- Rust nightly toolchain (matching the project's `rust-toolchain.toml`)
   686→- Node.js 22 for Playwright e2e tests
   687→- CI must pass before merging any pull request.
   688→
   689→### Work Tracking
   690→- **GitHub Issues**: For tracking features, bugs, and tasks visible to the project owner.
   691→- **Claude Task tool**: For in-progress coordination between team members during a session.
   692→- Issues and tasks should reference each other when applicable.
   693→
   694→---
   695→
   696→## 8. Team Composition Assessment
   697→
   698→### Current Team (9 members)
   699→| Name | Role | Domain |
   700→|------|------|--------|
   701→| Kent Beck | TDD Coach & Dev Practice Lead | TDD, XP, refactoring |
   702→| Scott Wlaschin | Domain Architect | DDD, type-driven design, FP |
   703→| Luca Palmieri | Lead Rust Engineer | Axum, SQLx, production Rust |
   704→| Carson Gross | Hypermedia Architect & Frontend Lead | HTMX, hypermedia systems |
   705→| Lea Verou | Frontend Engineer | CSS, web standards |
   706→| Steve Schoger | UI Designer | Visual design, design systems |
   707→| Steve Krug | UX Specialist | Usability, user advocacy |
   708→| Heydon Pickering | Accessibility Specialist | WCAG, inclusive design |
   709→| Marty Cagan | Product Manager | Product discovery, MVP |
   710→
   711→### Assessment
   712→The current team covers all necessary disciplines for this MVP:
   713→- **Product**: Marty Cagan
   714→- **Design**: Steve Schoger (visual) + Steve Krug (UX) + Heydon Pickering (a11y)
   715→- **Architecture**: Scott Wlaschin (domain) + Carson Gross (hypermedia) + Luca
   716→  Palmieri (Rust/infra)
   717→- **Frontend**: Carson Gross (HTMX) + Lea Verou (CSS/HTML) + Heydon Pickering
   718→  (a11y review)
   719→- **Backend**: Luca Palmieri + Scott Wlaschin
   720→- **Process**: Kent Beck (TDD, mob facilitation)
   721→
   722→### Potential Gaps to Monitor
   723→- **DevOps/Infrastructure**: No dedicated ops person. Luca has deployment experience
   724→  but if CI/CD or infrastructure becomes complex, we may want to recruit.
   725→- **Security**: Auth is in scope. Luca has experience but if we encounter complex
   726→  security requirements, consider a security specialist.
   727→- **QA/Testing beyond TDD**: The team practices TDD rigorously, but exploratory
   728→  testing and load testing may need attention for production readiness.
   729→- **Analytics/Measurement**: If we are serious about "outcomes over output," we will
   730→  eventually need to measure whether the product solves the user's problem. Structured
   731→  logging (`tracing`) is sufficient for MVP. Analytics instrumentation is a post-MVP gap.
   732→
   733→**Recommendation**: The current 9-person team is sufficient for the MVP. Revisit at
   734→the first retrospective after the core features are built.
   735→
   736→---
   737→
   738→## Signatures (Consensus Record)
   739→
   740→By agreeing to these terms, each team member commits to following these agreements
   741→and raising concerns through the established process when they feel the agreements
   742→need updating.
   743→
   744→- [x] Kent Beck
   745→- [x] Scott Wlaschin
   746→- [x] Luca Palmieri
   747→- [x] Carson Gross
   748→- [x] Lea Verou
   749→- [x] Steve Schoger
   750→- [x] Steve Krug
   751→- [x] Heydon Pickering
   752→- [x] Marty Cagan
   753→

# Team Agreements

*Formed: Day 0 -- Team Formation Session*
*Facilitator: Kent Beck*
*Status: APPROVED -- Consensus reached*

---

## 1. Working Agreements

### Core Principles
- **Outcomes over output**: We measure success by user problems solved, not features
  shipped. Before starting any feature, the team articulates the user problem it solves
  and how we will know it is working (success criteria).
- **MVP discipline is sacred**: Any idea beyond the defined MVP scope gets acknowledged,
  written on the Future List (`docs/future-ideas.md`), and deferred. The answer to
  "wouldn't it be cool if..." is "Yes -- in v2." Scope expansion requires team consensus,
  and Marty will push back hard unless there is a compelling discovery that changes our
  understanding of the user problem.
- **User stories are conversations, not contracts**: User stories guide our work but are
  owned by the whole team. Anyone can propose changes to acceptance criteria with good
  reasoning.

### Communication
- We communicate openly, directly, and respectfully.
- Every team member's perspective is valued equally -- the accessibility specialist's
  concerns carry the same weight as the lead engineer's.
- When raising a concern, propose an alternative. "I don't like X" is less useful than
  "I'd prefer Y because Z."
- We ask questions rather than make assumptions -- especially across disciplines.

### Driver-Reviewer Mob Model
- **Full 9-person mob is always active.** All team members participate in every task.
- **One Driver, eight Reviewers**: The Driver is the sole agent who may modify files
  (using Edit, Write, and Bash tools). All other agents operate as Reviewers -- they
  read code, review changes, and communicate feedback via messages only.
- **Non-driver agents MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files.**
  Violation is treated as a process failure.
- **Driver rotates by task**: The Driver is chosen based on the expertise needed for
  each task. The coordinator designates the Driver when assigning work.
- **Reviewers focus on their specialty**: Each Reviewer contributes from their area of
  expertise (a11y, UX, design, domain modeling, Rust patterns, HTMX, CSS, product,
  TDD discipline) and sends suggestions to the Driver via messages.
- **All suggestions flow through messages**: Reviewers describe intent and concerns,
  not keystrokes. The Driver translates feedback into code changes.
- **Everyone participates**: Designers, PM, accessibility specialist, domain architect --
  every perspective matters. Different eyes catch different things.
- **It's okay to say "I don't understand."** The mob is a learning environment.

### Driver Handoff Protocol
When a Driver is respawned (due to context loss, rotation, or session restart):
1. The coordinator provides a written summary of: what has been completed, what
   remains, and where to find the relevant code.
2. The new Driver reads the summary AND runs `git log --oneline -10` and
   `git diff HEAD` before writing any code.
3. The new Driver runs the full pipeline (fmt + clippy + test + playwright) to
   verify the current state is green before making changes.
4. The new Driver sends a "ready" message to the coordinator confirming they have
   context and a green baseline.

### Reviewer Coordination
- Before writing a detailed review, check if other reviewers have already flagged
  the same files. Build on their observations rather than duplicating. A brief "+1"
  is fine for agreement.
- Once a review is acknowledged, do not re-send it.

### Feature Workflow
Each feature follows this process with the full 9-person mob:

1. **Discovery**: Who is the user? What is the problem? What does "working"
   look like? Identify edge cases (empty state, error state, 1000 items, special
   characters). Marty leads.
2. **Type sketch**: What domain types and states are involved? Quick sketch of
   enums, newtypes, and workflows. Scott leads.
3. **Design intent**: What is the visual hierarchy? What states need designing
   (empty, loading, error, success)? Steve Schoger describes layout, spacing, and
   typography in concrete terms.
4. **TDD cycle**: Red-Green-Refactor. The compiler is our first test. Take the smallest
   step possible. The Driver writes code; Reviewers provide feedback via messages.
   Kent facilitates TDD discipline.
5. **Continuous review**: Reviewers actively review each change as it happens --
   accessibility checked by Heydon, usability checked by Steve Krug, design checked
   by Steve Schoger, hypermedia patterns checked by Carson, domain modeling checked
   by Scott, Rust patterns checked by Luca, product fit checked by Marty.
6. **Consensus gate**: After each atomic green step, all 9 agents confirm the change
   is correct before the Driver proceeds to the next step. The coordinator collects
   consensus before marking any task complete.

### Decision Cadence
- Day-to-day coding decisions (naming, small refactors, local patterns) are made
  naturally in the mob -- no formal process needed.
- Significant decisions (architecture, patterns, library choices, UX patterns, design
  system changes) use the consensus protocol defined in Section 4.

## 2. Definition of Done

A feature is **done** when ALL of the following are true:

### Product
- [ ] The user problem is clearly solved
- [ ] All acceptance criteria from the user story are met
- [ ] A first-time user can accomplish the task without hesitating (Trunk Test)
- [ ] User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature
      being delivered
- [ ] All navigation paths lead somewhere useful (no dead-end pages)
- [ ] Edge cases are handled gracefully (not just the happy path)
- [ ] The feature is deployable -- not just merged, but shippable to real users

### Engineering
- [ ] Tests are written first (TDD: Red-Green-Refactor) and all pass
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test` passes (unit + integration)
- [ ] `npx playwright test` passes -- browser-based e2e tests pass for affected
      user flows
- [ ] Error cases return meaningful HTML responses with structured logging
- [ ] Tracing spans cover the new functionality
- [ ] Database migrations (if any) are committed and `sqlx-data.json` is updated

### Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA)
- [ ] HTML is semantic and uses correct elements (`<button>`, `<label>`, `<ul>`, etc.)
- [ ] All form inputs have visible `<label>` elements (no placeholder-only inputs)
- [ ] All interactive elements are fully keyboard operable
- [ ] All interactive elements have unique accessible names including the item they
      act on (e.g., "Delete: Buy groceries", not just "Delete")
- [ ] Structural a11y verified via axe-core. Manual screen reader testing (VoiceOver,
      NVDA, or equivalent) for NEW interaction patterns (modals, live regions, focus
      management). Routine pages covered by axe-core don't require manual SR testing
      on every change
- [ ] ARIA used only when native HTML semantics are insufficient (with comments explaining why)
- [ ] Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI)
- [ ] Color is never the only indicator of state (always a secondary visual cue)
- [ ] Focus management is correct after HTMX swaps (see HTMX Focus Protocol below)
- [ ] Custom `:focus-visible` styles are present with sufficient contrast
- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` is respected for all animations/transitions

### Design
- [ ] Visual design follows the established design token system
- [ ] All states are designed: empty, loading, error, success
- [ ] Visual hierarchy is clear -- one primary focal point per screen
- [ ] Spacing is consistent and uses the defined scale

### Frontend
- [ ] Core functionality works via standard HTML forms and links (progressive
      enhancement -- HTMX and TypeScript enhance but are not required for basic operation)
- [ ] HTMX attributes follow locality of behavior (self-describing elements)
- [ ] TypeScript (if any) is non-critical and has accessible alternatives

### Process
- [ ] The mob has continuously reviewed code, design, usability, and accessibility
      during development
- [ ] All 9 team members have reviewed and consent to the completed work
- [ ] Labels and copy follow the Copy Standards (see Section 3)

## 3. Code Conventions and Patterns

### Project Structure

```
src/
  main.rs              -- Startup, configuration loading, server binding
  lib.rs               -- Re-exports for integration test access
  configuration.rs     -- Config structs, environment-aware loading
  startup.rs           -- Application builder (Router, DB pool, middleware)
  routes/              -- HTTP handlers (thin: extract, delegate, respond)
    mod.rs
    health_check.rs
  domain/              -- Pure domain types and logic (NO I/O)
    mod.rs
  services/            -- Application services (orchestrate domain + infra)
    mod.rs
  infrastructure/      -- Database queries, external service clients
    mod.rs
  templates/           -- Askama HTML templates
tests/
  api/                 -- Integration tests against the full HTTP stack
    mod.rs
    helpers.rs         -- TestApp, spawn_app(), shared fixtures
    health_check.rs
```

Dependencies flow inward: routes -> services -> domain + infrastructure.
Domain depends on nothing. This makes the domain layer trivially testable.

### Rust Conventions

**Type-driven domain modeling:**
- Newtypes for all domain identifiers and constrained values: `UserId(Uuid)`,
  `Email(String)`, `Title(String)`, `TodoItemId(Uuid)`
- Enums for state machines -- make illegal states unrepresentable:
  ```rust
  // GOOD: the type system prevents invalid states
  enum TodoItem {
      Pending { title: Title, created_at: DateTime<Utc> },
      Completed { title: Title, created_at: DateTime<Utc>, completed_at: DateTime<Utc> },
  }
  ```
- **Parse, don't validate**: Instead of `fn validate(input: &str) -> bool`, write
  `fn parse(input: String) -> Result<Email, ValidationError>`. Validation that returns
  a boolean throws away information. Parsing produces a typed value that carries the
  proof of validity. Validate once at the boundary; never re-validate inside the system.

**Workflows as type-safe pipelines:**
```
UnvalidatedInput -> ValidatedInput -> DomainEvent -> PersistenceResult
```
Each step's output type is the next step's input. You cannot skip validation or
persist before validating.

**Error handling (three layers):**
- **Domain errors**: Custom enum with `thiserror`. Domain terms only -- no HTTP concepts,
  no infrastructure types. A `TodoError::TitleEmpty` must never contain a `sqlx::Error`.
  Domain errors and infrastructure errors are separate types, translated at the boundary.
  ```rust
  #[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
  pub enum TodoError {
      #[error("Todo title cannot be empty")]
      TitleEmpty,
      #[error("Todo title too long (max {max}, got {actual})")]
      TitleTooLong { max: usize, actual: usize },
      #[error("Todo not found")]
      NotFound { id: TodoItemId },
      #[error(transparent)]
      Unexpected(#[from] anyhow::Error),
  }
  ```
- **Service errors**: Map domain errors to actionable responses. Add context.
- **Route errors**: Implement `IntoResponse` for error types. Return meaningful HTML
  (not raw 500s). Every error path produces a user-facing response.
- **Always add context** when propagating errors. A bare `?` that loses context is a
  code smell.
- **Log at error boundaries**: Every error crossing an architectural boundary gets
  logged with structured `tracing` fields.

**Idiomatic Rust:**
- Prefer iterators over loops, pattern matching over conditionals
- `?` operator for error propagation with context
- `#[must_use]` on important return types
- Compile-time guarantees: SQLx `query!`/`query_as!` macros, Askama templates

**Testing strategy (TDD):**
- **Unit tests** (`#[cfg(test)] mod tests`): Pure domain logic, validation, type
  conversions. Fast, no I/O, no mocking. **Litmus test: if you need a mock to test
  domain logic, I/O has leaked into the domain layer. Push it back to the boundary.**
- **Integration tests** (`tests/` directory): Full HTTP stack with real database.
  `TestApp` struct with `spawn_app()` helper. Unique database per test run
  (random name, run migrations, drop after).
- **No mocks for the database**: Use real PostgreSQL via Docker. Mocks give false
  confidence.
- **Test names as behavior specifications**:
  ```rust
  #[tokio::test]
  async fn registration_returns_422_when_email_is_missing() { ... }
  ```
- **Acceptance tests as usability statements**: "When I type a todo and press Enter,
  it appears in my list." Tests describe what the user expects to happen.
- **Property-based tests** with `proptest` where appropriate.
- **End-to-end tests** (`e2e/` directory): Playwright browser tests against the
  running application. Cover critical user journeys (register, login, core feature,
  logout). Use role-based and label-based locators (`getByRole`, `getByLabel`) so
  tests double as accessibility assertions. Keep this layer thin -- only add e2e
  tests for flows that integration tests cannot adequately verify.
- **Accessibility testing** (four layers):
  - *Automated*: axe-core (or equivalent) in the test suite for missing labels,
    contrast violations, and structural issues
  - *Keyboard*: Manual keyboard navigation testing for every interactive flow
  - *Screen reader*: Manual VoiceOver/NVDA testing for every feature
  - *Structural*: HTML validation for semantic correctness
  Tests can assert on roles, labels, and ARIA attributes -- documenting the expected
  screen reader experience as living specifications.

Note: Input validation will be handled through custom newtypes with validation in
